variables.L: Length of the pendulum string from pivot to centre of bob (m)
variables.θ: Angular displacement from the vertical (rad or deg)
variables.θ₀: Maximum angular displacement — amplitude (rad or deg)

locked_facts: Time period T = 2π√(L/g) — depends ONLY on length and gravitational acceleration
For small angles (θ < 15°), sin θ ≈ θ (in radians), making the motion simple harmonic
Restoring torque τ = −mgL sin θ ≈ −mgLθ for small angles, giving angular SHM with ω² = g/L
Period is INDEPENDENT of mass of the bob — mass cancels in the equation of motion
Period is INDEPENDENT of amplitude for small oscillations (isochronous property)
A seconds pendulum has T = 2 s and length L = g/π² ≈ 1 m on Earth's surface
truth_statements: The simple pendulum executes simple harmonic motion only for small angular displacements where sin θ can be approximated as θ
The time period depends on the length of the string and the value of g at that location, not on the mass or amplitude
At the lowest point of the swing, the bob has maximum kinetic energy and minimum potential energy
The effective length of a pendulum is measured from the point of suspension to the centre of mass of the bob
Measuring the time period of a simple pendulum provides an accurate method to determine the local value of g

aha_moment.what_to_show: Three pendulums side by side — same length but very different bob masses (50g, 200g, 500g). All three released simultaneously from the same angle, swinging in perfect synchrony. Period counters all show the same value.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Three visually distinct bobs (small, medium, large) swinging together in lock-step — the period readout above each is identical
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students directly see that mass has zero effect on the time period — the heavy bob does not swing faster or slower than the light one

simulation_states.label: Setup
simulation_states.description: A single pendulum at rest, displaced to angle θ₀ from the vertical. The string length L is labelled, the bob mass m is shown, the vertical equilibrium line is drawn, and the angle θ₀ is marked with an arc.
simulation_states.emphasis: Clearly identify the length L, mass m, gravitational acceleration g, and the initial displacement angle θ₀

simulation_states.label: Buildup
simulation_states.description: The pendulum swings back and forth. The restoring component mg sin θ is shown as a tangential arrow. A θ-t sinusoidal graph traces on the side. The period T is measured after one complete oscillation.
simulation_states.emphasis: The restoring force component is always directed toward the equilibrium (lowest point). The θ-t graph is sinusoidal for small angles.

simulation_states.label: Aha
simulation_states.description: Three pendulums appear — all same length L but with bob masses 50g, 200g, and 500g. All released from the same angle simultaneously. They swing in perfect synchrony. Period labels above each show identical T values.
simulation_states.emphasis: All three periods are identical regardless of mass. T = 2π√(L/g) contains no mass term. Mass cancels out in Newton's second law for the pendulum.

simulation_states.label: Explore
simulation_states.description: Interactive: adjust string length L, bob mass m, and initial angle θ₀. Period T updates in real time. Changing mass has zero effect on T. Changing L changes T. For large angles (>30°), the motion visibly deviates from SHM and period increases.
simulation_states.emphasis: Mass slider does not change T. Length slider changes T proportionally to √L. At large angles, the small-angle approximation breaks and motion is no longer perfectly sinusoidal.

common_misconceptions.belief: A heavier bob swings faster because gravity pulls it more strongly
trigger_phrases: bhari bob ka time period kam hoga
zyada mass zyada force toh zyada speed
common_misconceptions.correction: NCERT Ch. 13 derives T = 2π√(L/g) from the equation of motion. The mass m appears in both the restoring force (mg sin θ) and the inertia (ma), so it cancels. Heavier bobs have more force but also more inertia — the effects exactly cancel.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Three pendulums of different masses but same length swinging in perfect synchrony with identical period readouts

common_misconceptions.belief: The pendulum formula T = 2π√(L/g) works for any angle of swing
trigger_phrases: har angle pe formula kaam karega
any angle pe T = 2pi root L by g
common_misconceptions.correction: NCERT explicitly states the formula is valid only for small oscillations where sin θ ≈ θ (typically θ < 15°). For large angles, the actual period is longer than what the formula predicts, and the motion is not strictly simple harmonic.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Compare a small-angle pendulum (10°) with a large-angle one (50°) — the large-angle pendulum takes noticeably longer per swing, deviating from the T = 2π√(L/g) prediction

legend.meaning: Length of pendulum string (pivot to centre of bob)
Restoring force component mg sin θ
